attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with recurrent episodes of intermittent right iliac fossa pain over the past two weeks. She has a history of ileal Crohn's disease diagnosed three years ago but has not had formal review since then. She takes no regular medications and has a Mirena for contraception. She reports 5-6 loose bowel motions daily, compared to her usual 1-2 formed motions. There is no blood in her stools. She also describes fatigue but no weight loss.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits and is afebrile. Abdominal examination reveals no rebound tenderness, guarding or rigidity, with normal bowel sounds. Investigation results reveal:

  • Stool cultures: negative.

  • Urine pregnancy test: negative

  • Urinary dipstick: unremarkable.

  • White cell count: 15 × 10⁹/L

  • C-reactive protein: 28 mg/L (0-5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate first-line investigation to determine the extent of any small bowel disease?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms suggestive of a Crohn's disease flare after three years without treatment or follow-up. When assessing small bowel Crohn's disease, the choice of imaging modality is important, particularly in young patients who may require multiple investigations over their lifetime.

MR enterography is the most appropriate first-line investigation for this patient. It provides excellent visualisation of both the bowel wall and extraluminal complications without radiation exposure. This is particularly important in a young woman who will likely require repeated imaging throughout her life to monitor disease activity. MR enterography has comparable diagnostic accuracy to CT for assessing small bowel Crohn's disease, with the significant advantage of avoiding ionising radiation.

CT enterography would provide similar information about disease extent and complications but exposes the patient to significant radiation. Given the patient's young age and likelihood of requiring multiple imaging studies over her lifetime, the cumulative radiation exposure from repeated CT scans poses an unnecessary long-term risk. Additionally, CT scans carry potential risks in women of reproductive age who may have an unknown early pregnancy.

Push enteroscopy is an invasive procedure that allows direct visualisation of the proximal small bowel but has limited reach and would not adequately assess the entire small bowel. It provides only luminal information and would miss extraluminal complications such as fistulae or abscesses. This procedure requires sedation and carries risks of perforation and bleeding, making it inappropriate as a first-line investigation when non-invasive alternatives exist.

Laparoscopy is an invasive surgical procedure that would allow direct visualisation of the serosal surface of the bowel and other abdominal organs but provides limited information about mucosal disease. It requires general anesthesia and carries risks of complications including infection, bleeding, and future adhesions. This patient already has an increased risk of adhesions due to inflammatory bowel disease, and laparoscopy would further increase this risk. It is too invasive for first-line investigation when non-invasive imaging options are available.

Capsule endoscopy provides excellent mucosal views but has several limitations in this context. It gives no information about extraluminal pathology, which is important in assessing Crohn's disease complications. More importantly, there is a significant risk of capsule retention if strictures are present, which is common in Crohn's disease. The baseline risk of retention is 2.6%, increasing to 4-13% in symptomatic Crohn's disease. A patency capsule or cross-sectional imaging would be required before considering capsule endoscopy.
learningPoints: MR enterography is the preferred first-line investigation for small bowel Crohn's disease assessment in young patients to avoid radiation exposure.
Patients with Crohn's disease require regular monitoring to assess disease activity and extent, particularly after symptom changes.
references: Qiu Y, Mao R, Chen BL, Li XH, He Y, Zeng ZR, Li ZP, Chen MH. Systematic review with meta-analysis: magnetic resonance enterography vs. computed tomography enterography for evaluating disease activity in small bowel Crohn's disease. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2014 Jul;40(2):134-46. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24912799/

Jensen MD, Brodersen JB, Kjeldsen J. Capsule endoscopy for the diagnosis and follow up of Crohn's disease: a comprehensive review of current status. Ann Gastroenterol. 2017;30(2):168-178. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28243037/
